speaker: interviewer
text: And the date is December 7, 1995. Kurt, we left off on the last tape with you describing some little incidents of making it believable that you were really Americans because there were so many people that spoke German in your-- in your unit. And you had said at one point you dressed up in German uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German uniforms in order to demonstrate to our GIs what it would be like to encounter Germans in-- in combat and to acquaint them with the weapons that they were likely to find and how to use them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you were now in Northern Ireland, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your specific task in Northern Ireland was to do what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were, of course, waiting for the invasion to start. But we had been trained to interrogate prisoners of war for-- in our case, for tactical information that would be of immediate value to-- to our troops in knowing who opposed them and in what strength and-- and with what weapons.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in Northern Ireland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during this time, you were still undergoing training or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, we were marking time, of course. But we were trying-- we-- we ourselves received some training, but more so was the fact that we tried to train our troops and acquaint them with everything that the German army represented.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, during this time, were you still receiving mail from your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- just prior to my going in the army, I had received my last few letters that I had sent to my parents to the south of France, where they were deported in 1940 on roughly half an hour's notice to-- and--. But we still had been in touch with them and-- and had tried to get them out of there. And a few times it looked as though we might get them out of there. But the authorities really made it very difficult. And then also, the events overtook us again when Pearl Harbor happened and set us back to square one. It was extremely difficult to get to be asked to come to the American consulate, which their nearest one was Marseille. And when you did so, you weren't assured of getting a visa. But you had to have proof that you had all the necessary exit visas from the other countries concerned, which meant France and Spain and sometimes Portugal, because that's how they would have left, had they been able to leave. And also, you had to show ships' reservations, and very often those ships would sail. And-- and my parents hadn't even been asked to appear before the American consul-- consul in Marseille. So to get that timing right and to always be ready with reservations, it was just one frustration after another. And so, as I was saying, the-- last-- this went on until May of '42. And eventually, later in the-- in the fall of '42, before I went in the army, some of the letters that I had sent to them came back saying, 'Address Unknown,' 'Left No Forwarding Address.' And-- so we knew that we could fear the worst, although we didn't know, of course, exactly what had happened. So I was well aware of, you know, what I probably had to contend with. I believe you asked me a question about whether I was still in touch with my parents once I was in the army. The answer to that is no. And the biggest irony of all is that-- that in November, after I was in the army in November of '42, we received word from the State Department that they had advised their consulate in Marseille to grant visas to my parents. The only irony of that was that my parents had been deported to Auschwitz two months earlier, as we found out after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you ship out from Ireland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I shipped out right after the invasion started, June 6. We went to a staging area in-- in the south of England. And from there, we, you know, went on-- on these-- went on the invasion fleet. I mean, we-- we came after the invasion had already begun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you-- where did you land?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I landed on-- it was called Utah Sugar Red, which-- Utah Beach. The nearest town that is well known was Sainte-Mere-Eglise, and where we dug in-- actually took over some of the foxholes left by the previous division that we relieved. And we were sort of-- we had to stay there until the breakthrough at Saint-Lo, which was-- I believe was early in July, after which we were on the move, you know. And so we went through the Normandy campaign and then went somewhat south to a city called Angers. And from there we went to the city of Lance in-- more-- more in the east of France. And from there to-- well, we saw, of course, the liberation of Paris, although I myself was not in Paris but some 20 miles outside of Paris at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you actively engaged in battle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was actively engaged, but I-- that-- that depends exactly how you mean that. My job was to interrogate prisoners of war. I-- I-- I-- I was not at that time doing any shooting myself, but-- but-- but we were of course exposed to all the same mortar fire, artillery fire, and-- and anything else that anybody else was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you dealing with any POWs at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of your experiences during this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, you know-- we found that some of those prisoners, in the earlier days especially, were still somewhat arrogant. But we-- we usually were able to-- to get some information out of them. And also, by interrogating many of them, and we could play some of them up against the others-- you know, that so-and-so said such and such about your unit. How about that? You-- you learn how to extract certain information that-- that help you to put the pieces together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what major battles was your unit involved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- they were mainly the-- the battles of-- for those cities that I mentioned-- Angers-- well, then first the breakthrough in Saint-Lo, then Angers, then Reims, then Verdun. And-- and then, by-- by fall of '44, we were-- we had-- you know, I was with Patton's Third Army. And incidentally, I met General Patton, and he even posed for me. I-- I took some pictures of him. I-- I chatted with him for a brief while. And that was quite an experience. But we-- we actually came to a halt in the city of Metz, because we had run out of gas, and we had to wait because General Patton had gone so fast that some of the supplies couldn't keep up. Also, there weren't enough of them, so we had to sort of regroup. So we spent a considerable amount of time in-- in the city of Metz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit more about your conversation with General Patton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, occasionally, he would-- he would come to visit, to-- to check on what was going on. And first of all, I can only say that he was one general that was in any battle, did not shirk danger. He-- he was right there with the troops, which instilled a great deal of confidence in the troops, and even-- and despite his profanity and the kind of pep talks that he was so well known for, we sort of took that in almost in a humorous vein, and-- and the-- and the GIs had a lot of respect for him. Some of the things that you've heard about him is that he-- he was a little bit vain, perhaps, and was always seeking glory. So that when I met him, and he came to our regimental headquarters, he saw that I had a camera. And he-- he-- he said to me, wait, wait, let me adjust my-- my trench coat, and so on. If I had known you were going to take pictures of me today, I would have worn a better uniform. [LAUGHS] And he-- he was quite congenial.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk a little bit more about the interrogation process. Can-- can you elaborate more on that, of the POWs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we were, of course, taught to some degree, you know, how-- also how not to ask questions so that we-- we were able to compile, usually from a number of prisoners, what we-- what we were immediately looking for that would make our-- our troops' lives a little safer and-- and better, and also to effectively knock them out. For instance, we would find out where their observation posts were and-- and, you know-- and where-- where some of their troops were-- were, you know, reinforcing certain lines. We knew that if there was concentration, a heavier concentration of troops, we knew that there they were doing something either in a defensive position or possibly for-- for offense. And so-- so you learned, you know, how to ask the right questions. And by-- also by finding out what units they were with, you knew, for instance, what-- what troops the Germans had had to-- to bring in, say, from another front, even from the Russian front sometimes, because once in a while, we would discover a new unit that we knew wasn't supposed to be there. Then we-- then we knew they were reinforcing their lines from that, or that they had had so many losses that they had to bring in other reinforcements.

speaker: interviewer
text: How easy was it for you to extrapolate the information from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it-- to-- to varying degrees. It-- it-- it could be relatively easy, and it could be extremely hard. Sometimes some of the prisoners wanted to ingratiate themselves also with us and-- and-- and would appear to give information rather freely, whereas others, and especially some of the SS troops, were a lot harder to break down. And we really had no time to-- to-- to stay with them for a-- for a very long time and to do interrogation in depth, because the battle conditions didn't allow that. We tried to get the-- the-- the information immediately of use to us and our troops. And-- and that we normally were able to get.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the prisoners housed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the prisoners were brought in, of course, by-- by our troops to-- to our, you know, regimental headquarters. And this being summer, it was no problem about housing them. We had-- we had regular enclosures for them that our MPs had made for just that purpose, in whatever place we happened to be. And-- but from those positions, they would soon thereafter be evacuated to the rear, where they had real prisoner-of-war enclosures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you interrogate anybody that was infamous?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not-- not this early. But later on, toward the end of the war, our troops brought in someone. And-- and when I asked, you know, where he had been, he-- he said, well, he had been in Berlin in recent times. And when I-- when I pressed him for further information, he quite readily admitted that he was, in fact, Hitler's chauffeur, had been in the bunker in which Hitler and Eva Braun committed suicide. That in fact, Hitler had called him in to give him instructions as to how to dispose of their bodies. And those instructions, he said, he carried out to the letter. Hitler had told him that he was to take the bodies to the courtyard of the bunker and pour cans of gas over them and set them aflame, and make sure that-- that there would be no remains or as few remains as possible, so that they could not be desecrated. Or the Russians-- they expected the Russians to take this soon, that they would make something of that and use it for whatever the Russians would have used it for. And-- and he-- he claims to have carried out those instructions to the letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, his name was Erich Kempka-- K-E-M-P, I think, K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he readily give you the information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because by that time, this was an accomplished fact, and he had really nothing to hide anymore. And as I was to read many years thereafter, this is exactly the story he gave to other historians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he describe to you the bunker or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he described it, but I suppose I didn't even ask him too much about the bunker because I-- it wasn't any-- anything I could have done with that information. And at any rate, I-- I-- I didn't have time to go into such details.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, now, I know that you said that happened a little bit later. So backtracking again--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the last major battle that you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we were in-- in Metz, at the time. And surrounding the hills around Metz were mountains on which there were some forts in which the Germans held out. So we had to-- we had to secure those forts. And we tried for-- it took quite a number of weeks till we could neutralize them. And then finally, the forts were ready to surrender. And I-- I remember going in there to take the surrender of the forts. And one thing I remember about-- actually, I was involved in two forts-- going with a German officer and walking first on the outside of the fort and seeing the cadavers of horses that had-- that had been killed in the shelling, you know, and all that, and this German officer bemoaning those poor animals, but having had absolutely no mercy, quite obviously. So he was more concerned about those horses than about human lives. And when I went inside, I found there was a colonel in charge. And after we exchanged the usual formalities and-- and I had time to talk to him, one of the first things he wanted to know from me-- this was now in probably November, November of '44-- he, of course, also bemoaned that it was necessary, you know, all this killing and so on. But he wanted to know, or he suggested that the logical thing would be for us to team up with them-- the Germans-- against the Russians. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I-- I asked him, you know, I-- I-- I asked him some questions about the-- you know, he-- he said something to me about, you know, why It was necessary for us to have taken this town or that town. And I said, well, you-- you didn't seem to mind taking these towns and-- and-- and-- and even, you know, bombing them-- Rotterdam and Coventry and all those places. And-- and-- and you didn't seem to mind-- because he tried to tell me, you know, how Germany didn't really want some of these things. I said, you didn't seem to mind your conquests. And he said, well, I have to admit one thing. The-- the-- there's a German proverb that goes something like, the appetite grows with the eating. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So he at least admitted that much. And that was just one of the experiences along the way. After Metz, we-- we finally, you know-- and after those forts surrendered, we finally were able to push on. And we actually crossed into Germany, so far still on-- on the-- on the west side of-- of the Rhine. But then we-- we got to the Rhine in-- in March of '45 and were able to cross it in a town called Nierstein. And from then on, things developed pretty rapidly. But of course, at first, there was still, in the winter of-- of '44, '45 was, of course, the Battle of the Bulge. At that time we were in Alsace-Lorraine, the Third Army. And when the Germans went on the offensive, we were called as reinforcements and accomplished that move in-- really in record time, and to bolster our defenses against the-- the Germans. And fortunately, were able to stem their advance. And when-- once the weather cleared and our Air Force could start their bombings and strafings, of course it became a lot better. And we stopped the Battle of the Bulge. And then, as I said, from there we went to Germany, at first to the rural area, a town called Iserlohn. And then we also advanced on the-- on the city of Frankfurt, and-- and we took that city. And later on, you know, we crossed-- we crossed such-- before then, we crossed such well-known places as Bitburg, for instance, and-- until we came to the Rhine and crossed it. And our main objective then became Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all this time, you were still in intelligence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interrogating more and more POWs as you went along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And of course, you could see the German defenses crumbling at that point. But, you know, they were still, of course-- some of them were diehards, and they just didn't want to-- to give up. They also-- the thing that invariably came up in their-- in their interrogation was that-- that they, the Germans, were working on secret weapons that would allow them to win-- to still win the war. And of course, some of this was-- was to some-- at one point, when we kept hearing those stories, we couldn't know for sure because there were, in fact, the V-1s and V-2s, and we didn't know what else they might have up their sleeves. So we-- we learned to-- not to pooh-pooh these things. But quite obviously, they had been fed a lot of propaganda about the so-called secret weapon. But I also found out for the first time about jet fighters, you know, which-- which they had in those days. And so we didn't know really what-- what else might show up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how-- what else happened? Where were you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once we left Frankfurt and-- we-- we were called to the border area between Germany and Czechoslovakia. We were-- we were to link up with the Russians, who were coming through Czechoslovakia, you know, in the opposite direction. And so I remember getting to a town called Eleonorenhain in-- which was in the so-called Sudeten area, which was the border between Germany and-- and Czechoslovakia. And in those towns, the populace was mostly Sudeten Germans rather than Czechs. And we were at first mired in the mud for-- for a number of days in this town of Eleonorenhain. We couldn't advance because of incessant rains. And I-- in that town, I think I also remember hearing about President Roosevelt's death, in our case, on April 13. I believe he died on the 12th, but it was already the 13th when I heard it. And as soon as the weather improved, though, we-- we knew we were ready for our advance. And we-- reports had reached us that a-- a town about five, six miles away from-- from there was ready to surrender. They were flying white flags.

speaker: interviewee
text: So three jeeps were assigned to go in there and take the surrender. One jeep was the military intelligence officer and his driver, who would take the actual civilian surrender. The second one was a medical officer and his driver to see what we might find there that needed attention.

speaker: interviewee
text: The third one was me, who had to go-- I had to search for any possible remnants of German troops in that town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- so we set out. We were really just six people that set out to-- to get to that town. And on the road, those four, five, six miles, I remember there-- there was still shelling going on from the other side of town. There were some German artillery units that-- that were shelling us. And I remember driving along and looking down to the side of the jeep and seeing a shell drop right next to the jeep, and then rolling over into the ditch. And I hesitate to think what would have happened to me and this family had this shell not turned out to be a dud.
